text: Hello, good morning. This is a case of laparoscopic myomectomy. This patient has left sided broad ligament myoma and we will do laparoscopic myomectomy.

The surgeon injects vasopressin mixed with normal saline into the dissection plane, preparing to address the hydronephrosis caused by the pressure of the left-sided broad ligament myoma on the ureter.

The surgeon performs hydro dissection by injecting a solution of vasopressin diluted with normal saline into the tissue plane, facilitating separation of surrounding tissue and connective tissue without damaging collateral tissues.
The surgeon separates the encapsulated myoma, which is clearly visible. A myoma screw is positioned on the left side of the port, and the surgeon begins retracting the myoma using the screw.

The surgeon uses two myoma screws to provide bilateral symmetrical traction on the myoma in a nulliparous patient, ensuring optimal manipulation without a uterine manipulator. After applying the second screw, the first screw is withdrawn.

The surgeon carefully separates the base of the myoma from surrounding structures, avoiding abrupt avulsion to prevent vessel damage. The ureters are meticulously separated, and the myoma is easily extracted due to prior hydrodissection.
The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator and a morselator to perform morselation on the broad ligament myoma, a process that involves gradually cutting and removing the myoma tissue.
The surgeon uses a morselator to gradually remove the myoma in small pieces, a process that typically takes fifteen to twenty minutes.
So, it was a simple case of the left-sided broadly governed myoma. Thank you for watching this video.
